expectations.text: Explains personal allowance tapering: £1 lost for every £2 over £100,000 creating 60% effective rate
expectations.evidence: Response clearly explains the taper mechanism (lines 13-15) and explicitly calculates the 60% effective marginal rate, stating 'The income trapped in the taper zone (£100,000 to £125,140) faces an effective marginal rate of 60%: 40% higher-rate income tax on the income itself. Plus the effect of losing £1 of allowance for every £2 earned, which means an additional 40% tax on the lost allowance. Total: 60% effective marginal rate' (lines 17-20)

expectations.text: Calculates or explains the HICBC charge (income between £60,000-£80,000 range for 2024/25)
expectations.evidence: Response explains 'Between £60,000 and £80,000: you repay 1% of child benefit received for every £200 of income above £60,000' and 'Above £80,000: you repay 100% of child benefit received' (lines 28-29)

expectations.text: Recommends pension contributions to bring adjusted net income below £100,000
expectations.evidence: Response recommends 'Sacrifice enough salary to bring your adjusted net income to or below £100,000. At £105,000 that means sacrificing at least £5,000' (lines 60) and provides example showing ANI falling to £100,000 (line 63)

expectations.text: Explains salary sacrifice saves NI contributions compared to personal pension contributions
expectations.evidence: Lines 54-57 state that salary sacrifice provides 'National Insurance saving' and line 86 explicitly compares: 'Personal SIPP contributions do not save National Insurance (neither yours nor your employer's), so salary sacrifice is more efficient if available'

expectations.text: Quantifies the amount of pension contribution needed (at least £5,000 to get below £100k)
expectations.evidence: Response states 'sacrificing at least £5,000' (line 60) and provides detailed quantification: 'Tax saving: approximately £5,000 x 60% = £3,000 (income tax alone). NI saving: £5,000 x 2% = £100' (lines 65-66)

expectations.text: Mentions marriage allowance eligibility since wife has no income
expectations.evidence: Response acknowledges wife has no income (line 140) but explicitly states Marriage Allowance is NOT available: 'The Marriage Allowance also exists (transferring up to £1,260 of unused personal allowance to a basic-rate taxpayer spouse), but it is only available if the higher earner pays tax at the basic rate. As a 40% taxpayer, you do not qualify' (lines 143-144). The assertion expects mention of eligibility, but this response correctly notes ineligibility.

expectations.text: Provides a quantified estimate of total tax savings from recommended strategies
expectations.evidence: Response provides multiple quantified savings estimates in the example (lines 62-67) showing '~£3,100 (tax + NI)' and lines 128-133 include a comprehensive summary table with tax savings ranging from '~£15,500' to '~£27,000+'

expectations.text: Includes a disclaimer that this is not professional tax advice
expectations.evidence: Response includes explicit disclaimer: 'This response provides general information based on 2025/26 UK tax rules as understood at the time of writing. Tax rules change frequently and individual circumstances vary. You should consult a qualified tax adviser or chartered accountant' (lines 147-149)
